525→func (s preparedGenericAPIServer) RunWithContext(ctx context.Context) error {
   526→	stopCh := ctx.Done()
   527→	delayedStopCh := s.lifecycleSignals.AfterShutdownDelayDuration
   528→	shutdownInitiatedCh := s.lifecycleSignals.ShutdownInitiated
   529→
   530→	// Clean up resources on shutdown.
   531→	defer s.Destroy()
   532→
   533→	// If UDS profiling is enabled, start a local http server listening on that socket
   534→	if s.UnprotectedDebugSocket != nil {
   535→		go func() {
   536→			defer utilruntime.HandleCrashWithContext(ctx)
   537→			klog.Error(s.UnprotectedDebugSocket.RunWithContext(ctx))
   538→		}()
   539→	}
   540→
   541→	// spawn a new goroutine for closing the MuxAndDiscoveryComplete signal
   542→	// registration happens during construction of the generic api server
   543→	// the last server in the chain aggregates signals from the previous instances
   544→	go func() {
   545→		for _, muxAndDiscoveryCompletedSignal := range s.GenericAPIServer.MuxAndDiscoveryCompleteSignals() {
   546→			select {
   547→			case <-muxAndDiscoveryCompletedSignal:
   548→				continue
   549→			case <-stopCh:
   550→				klog.V(1).Infof("haven't completed %s, stop requested", s.lifecycleSignals.MuxAndDiscoveryComplete.Name())
   551→				return
   552→			}
   553→		}
   554→		s.lifecycleSignals.MuxAndDiscoveryComplete.Signal()
   555→		klog.V(1).Infof("%s has all endpoints registered and discovery information is complete", s.lifecycleSignals.MuxAndDiscoveryComplete.Name())
   556→	}()
   557→
   558→	go func() {
   559→		defer delayedStopCh.Signal()
   560→		defer klog.V(1).InfoS("[graceful-termination] shutdown event", "name", delayedStopCh.Name())
   561→
   562→		<-stopCh
   563→
   564→		// As soon as shutdown is initiated, /readyz should start returning failure.
   565→		// This gives the load balancer a window defined by ShutdownDelayDuration to detect that /readyz is red
   566→		// and stop sending traffic to this server.
   567→		shutdownInitiatedCh.Signal()
   568→		klog.V(1).InfoS("[graceful-termination] shutdown event", "name", shutdownInitiatedCh.Name())
   569→
   570→		time.Sleep(s.ShutdownDelayDuration)
   571→	}()
   572→
   573→	// close socket after delayed stopCh
   574→	shutdownTimeout := s.ShutdownTimeout
   575→	if s.ShutdownSendRetryAfter {
   576→		// when this mode is enabled, we do the following:
   577→		// - the server will continue to listen until all existing requests in flight
   578→		//   (not including active long running requests) have been drained.
   579→		// - once drained, http Server Shutdown is invoked with a timeout of 2s,
   580→		//   net/http waits for 1s for the peer to respond to a GO_AWAY frame, so
   581→		//   we should wait for a minimum of 2s
   582→		shutdownTimeout = 2 * time.Second
   583→		klog.V(1).InfoS("[graceful-termination] using HTTP Server shutdown timeout", "shutdownTimeout", shutdownTimeout)
   584→	}
   585→
   586→	notAcceptingNewRequestCh := s.lifecycleSignals.NotAcceptingNewRequest
   587→	drainedCh := s.lifecycleSignals.InFlightRequestsDrained
   588→	// Canceling the parent context does not immediately cancel the HTTP server.
   589→	// We only inherit context values here and deal with cancellation ourselves.
   590→	stopHTTPServerCtx, stopHTTPServer := context.WithCancelCause(context.WithoutCancel(ctx))
   591→	go func() {
   592→		defer stopHTTPServer(errors.New("time to stop HTTP server"))
   593→
   594→		timeToStopHttpServerCh := notAcceptingNewRequestCh.Signaled()
   595→		if s.ShutdownSendRetryAfter {
   596→			timeToStopHttpServerCh = drainedCh.Signaled()
   597→		}
   598→
   599→		<-timeToStopHttpServerCh
   600→	}()
   601→
   602→	// Start the audit backend before any request comes in. This means we must call Backend.Run
   603→	// before http server start serving. Otherwise the Backend.ProcessEvents call might block.
   604→	// AuditBackend.Run will stop as soon as all in-flight requests are drained.
   605→	if s.AuditBackend != nil {
   606→		if err := s.AuditBackend.Run(drainedCh.Signaled()); err != nil {
   607→			return fmt.Errorf("failed to run the audit backend: %v", err)
   608→		}
   609→	}
   610→
   611→	stoppedCh, listenerStoppedCh, err := s.NonBlockingRunWithContext(stopHTTPServerCtx, shutdownTimeout)
   612→	if err != nil {
   613→		return err
   614→	}
   615→
   616→	httpServerStoppedListeningCh := s.lifecycleSignals.HTTPServerStoppedListening
   617→	go func() {
   618→		<-listenerStoppedCh
   619→		httpServerStoppedListeningCh.Signal()
   620→		klog.V(1).InfoS("[graceful-termination] shutdown event", "name", httpServerStoppedListeningCh.Name())
   621→	}()
   622→
   623→	// we don't accept new request as soon as both ShutdownDelayDuration has
   624→	// elapsed and preshutdown hooks have completed.

discovery information is complete\", s.lifecycleSignals.MuxAndDiscoveryComplete.Name())\n\t}()\n\n\tgo func() {\n\t\tdefer delayedStopCh.Signal()\n\t\tdefer klog.V(1).InfoS(\"[graceful-termination] shutdown event\", \"name\", delayedStopCh.Name())\n\n\t\t<-stopCh\n\n\t\t// As soon as shutdown is initiated, /readyz should start returning failure.\n\t\t// This gives the load balancer a window defined by ShutdownDelayDuration to detect that /readyz is red\n\t\t// and stop sending traffic to this server.\n\t\tshutdownInitiatedCh.Signal()\n\t\tklog.V(1).InfoS(\"[graceful-termination] shutdown event\", \"name\", shutdownInitiatedCh.Name())\n\n\t\ttime.Sleep(s.ShutdownDelayDuration)\n\t}()\n\n\t// close socket after delayed stopCh\n\tshutdownTimeout := s.ShutdownTimeout\n\tif s.ShutdownSendRetryAfter {\n\t\t// when this mode is enabled, we do the following:\n\t\t// - the server will continue to listen until all existing requests in flight\n\t\t//   (not including active long running requests) have been drained.\n\t\t// - once drained, http Server Shutdown is invoked with a timeout of 2s,\n\t\t//   net/http waits for 1s for the peer to respond to a GO_AWAY frame, so\n\t\t//   we should wait for a minimum of 2s\n\t\tshutdownTimeout = 2 * time.Second\n\t\tklog.V(1).InfoS(\"[graceful-termination] using HTTP Server shutdown timeout\", \"shutdownTimeout\", shutdownTimeout)\n\t}\n\n\tnotAcceptingNewRequestCh := s.lifecycleSignals.NotAcceptingNewRequest\n\tdrainedCh := s.lifecycleSignals.InFlightRequestsDrained\n\t// Canceling the parent context does not immediately cancel the HTTP server.\n\t// We only inherit context values here and deal with cancellation ourselves.\n\tstopHTTPServerCtx, stopHTTPServer := context.WithCancelCause(context.WithoutCancel(ctx))\n\tgo func() {\n\t\tdefer stopHTTPServer(errors.New(\"time to stop HTTP server\"))\n\n\t\ttimeToStopHttpServerCh := notAcceptingNewRequestCh.Signaled()\n\t\tif s.ShutdownSendRetryAfter {\n\t\t\ttimeToStopHttpServerCh = drainedCh.Signaled()\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t<-timeToStopHttpServerCh\n\t}()\n\n\t// Start the audit backend before any request comes in. This means we must call Backend.Run\n\t// before http server start serving. Otherwise the Backend.ProcessEvents call might block.\n\t// AuditBackend.Run will stop as soon as all in-flight requests are drained.\n\tif s.AuditBackend != nil {\n\t\tif err := s.AuditBackend.Run(drainedCh.Signaled()); err != nil {\n\t\t\treturn fmt.Errorf(\"failed to run the audit backend: %v\", err)\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\tstoppedCh, listenerStoppedCh, err := s.NonBlockingRunWithContext(stopHTTPServerCtx, shutdownTimeout)\n\tif err != nil {\n\t\treturn err\n\t}\n\n\thttpServerStoppedListeningCh := s.lifecycleSignals.HTTPServerStoppedListening\n\tgo func() {\n\t\t<-listenerStoppedCh\n\t\thttpServerStoppedListeningCh.Signal()\n\t\tklog.V(1).InfoS(\"[graceful-termination] shutdown event\", \"name\", httpServerStoppedListeningCh.Name())\n\t}()\n\n\t// we don't accept new request as soon as both ShutdownDelayDuration has\n\t// elapsed and preshutdown hooks have completed.", "numLines": 100, "startLine": 525, "totalLines": 1091}}

arguments.content:
# Kubernetes API Server Library: Architecture and Extension Guide

## 1. Subsystem Purpose and Boundaries

The `apiserver` library (`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/`) is a reusable framework for building Kubernetes-style API servers. It provides the full HTTP request lifecycle—authentication, authorization, admission control, serialization, REST semantics, storage, and watch—as composable Go packages. The `kube-apiserver` binary is the primary consumer, but the library is intentionally decoupled so that projects like the aggregated API server, CRD API server, and custom API servers built with `apiserver-builder` can embed and extend it independently.

### Boundaries

The library owns everything *between* the TLS listener and the etcd client. It does **not** own:

- **Upstream:** TLS certificate management, load-balancer health probing, or network policy. These are external to the process.
- **Downstream:** The etcd cluster itself. The library provides an etcd3 storage driver (`pkg/storage/etcd3/`) but treats etcd as a pluggable backend behind the `storage.Interface` abstraction (`pkg/storage/interfaces.go:169`).
- **Peer concerns:** Controller logic, scheduler decisions, and kubelet communication. The API server library is strictly a data-plane gateway for the cluster's persisted state.

### Upstream Dependencies

| Dependency | Role |
|---|---|
| `k8s.io/apimachinery` | Shared type system (`runtime.Object`, `schema.GroupVersionKind`), serialization codecs, field selectors, label selectors |
| `k8s.io/client-go` | Loopback client for self-referential operations (post-start hooks use `LoopbackClientConfig`) |
| `go.etcd.io/etcd/client/v3` | etcd gRPC client used by `pkg/storage/etcd3/store.go` |
| `github.com/emicklei/go-restful/v3` | HTTP routing framework for API group endpoints |
| `k8s.io/kube-openapi` | OpenAPI v2/v3 spec generation and serving |

### Downstream Consumers

Any binary that calls `GenericAPIServer.InstallAPIGroups()` to register resources. The canonical example is `cmd/kube-apiserver`, but the library is also used by the aggregator (`kube-aggregator`) and sample API servers.

---

## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split

The library decomposes into six major subsystems. Each has a clear responsibility boundary and communicates with neighbors through well-defined Go interfaces.

### 2.1 Server Core (`pkg/server/`)

**Responsibility:** Assembles the HTTP handler chain, manages server lifecycle (start, readiness, graceful shutdown), and provides the delegation mechanism for composing multiple API servers.

**Key types:**

- `GenericAPIServer` (`genericapiserver.go:109`): The central server struct. Holds the handler, admission controller, authorizer, post-start hooks, health checks, lifecycle signals, and the delegation target chain.
- `Config` (`config.go:110`): Configuration for building a `GenericAPIServer`. Contains `BuildHandlerChainFunc`, authentication/authorization info, flow control settings, shutdown parameters, and the `RESTOptionsGetter` for storage configuration.
- `APIServerHandler` (`handler.go:42`): Wraps three HTTP handler tiers: `FullHandlerChain` (all filters applied), `GoRestfulContainer` (API routes), and `NonGoRestfulMux` (non-API routes like `/healthz`, `/metrics`). The `Director` (`handler.go:115`) routes between the two backends.
- `DelegationTarget` interface (`genericapiserver.go:308`): Enables chaining of multiple API servers (e.g., kube-apiserver delegates to aggregator delegates to apiextensions-server). Each delegate contributes its own hooks, health checks, and unprotected handlers.

### 2.2 Filter Chain (`pkg/server/filters/`, `pkg/endpoints/filters/`)

**Responsibility:** Implements the ordered chain of HTTP middleware that every request passes through before reaching the REST handler.

These are pure `http.Handler` wrappers composed in `DefaultBuildHandlerChain()` (`config.go:1015`). The chain is assembled inside-out (innermost handler wraps first), so the outermost filter in the source code is the first to execute at runtime.

Filter files are split across two packages:
- `pkg/server/filters/` — infrastructure filters (panic recovery, timeouts, flow control, CORS, GOAWAY)
- `pkg/endpoints/filters/` — API-semantic filters (authentication, authorization, audit, impersonation, request info)

### 2.3 Endpoint Handlers (`pkg/endpoints/`)

**Responsibility:** Maps REST verbs to handler functions, installs API group routes into the go-restful container, and drives content negotiation.

- `APIInstaller` (`installer.go:62`): Given an `APIGroupVersion` with a storage map, generates go-restful `WebService` routes for every resource. The central function `registerResourceHandlers()` (`installer.go:287`) introspects which `rest.*` interfaces each storage implements (e.g., `rest.Creater`, `rest.Lister`, `rest.Watcher`) and registers only the supported verbs.
- Verb handlers in `pkg/endpoints/handlers/`: `GetResource()` / `ListResource()` (`get.go`), `CreateResource()` (`create.go`), `UpdateResource()` (`update.go`), `PatchResource()` (`patch.go`), `DeleteResource()` / `DeleteCollection()` (`delete.go`), `WatchServer` (`watch.go`).
- Content negotiation (`handlers/negotiation/negotiate.go`): Matches `Accept` / `Content-Type` headers to registered serializers (JSON, YAML, Protobuf, CBOR).

### 2.4 Admission Control (`pkg/admission/`)

**Responsibility:** Runs pluggable mutation and validation logic on create, update, and delete operations after authentication and authorization but before persistence.

- `Interface` / `MutationInterface` / `ValidationInterface` (`interfaces.go:123-144`): The core plugin interfaces. A plugin can implement mutation, validation, or both.
- `chainAdmissionHandler` (`chain.go:23`): Chains multiple plugins. `Admit()` runs all mutating plugins in order, short-circuiting on error. `Validate()` runs all validating plugins and aggregates errors.
- Admission is invoked inside endpoint handlers. For example, in `CreateResource()` (`create.go`), mutating admission runs at line ~203 via `mutatingAdmission.Admit()`, and validating admission is wired through `rest.AdmissionToValidateObjectFunc()`.

### 2.5 Authentication & Authorization (`pkg/authentication/`, `pkg/authorization/`, `pkg/endpoints/filters/`)

**Responsibility:** Establish caller identity and enforce access policy.

- **Authentication filter** (`endpoints/filters/authentication.go:46`): `WithAuthentication()` calls `authenticator.Request.AuthenticateRequest()`, stores the resulting `user.Info` in the request context, and strips the `Authorization` header. If authentication fails, the request is forwarded to a `failedHandler` that returns 401.
- **Authorization filter** (`endpoints/filters/authorization.go:53`): `WithAuthorization()` constructs `AttributesRecord` from the request info and user, calls `authorizer.Authorize()`, and returns 403 on `DecisionDeny` or `DecisionNoOpinion`. The `Authorizer` interface (`authorization/authorizer/interfaces.go:83`) returns a three-valued decision: `DecisionAllow`, `DecisionDeny`, `DecisionNoOpinion`.

### 2.6 Storage Layer (`pkg/storage/`, `pkg/registry/`)

**Responsibility:** Provides a key-value abstraction over etcd and an in-memory watch cache for serving list and watch requests efficiently.

- `storage.Interface` (`storage/interfaces.go:169`): The backend contract. Methods: `Create`, `Delete`, `Watch`, `Get`, `GetList`, `GuaranteedUpdate`. Implementations do not know about Kubernetes resource semantics—they operate on opaque keys and `runtime.Object`.
- `etcd3.store` (`storage/etcd3/store.go:80`): Direct etcd3 implementation. Handles codec transformations, lease management, and value encryption via a configurable `value.Transformer`.
- `Cacher` (`storage/cacher/cacher.go:262`): In-memory cache that fronts the etcd3 store. Serves `Watch` and `List` from a ring-buffer `watchCache` populated by a `Reflector`. Mutations are delegated to the underlying `storage.Interface`. This is the component that makes watch scalable—without it, every watch would be a direct etcd watch, which does not scale past a few hundred concurrent watchers.
- `Store` (`registry/generic/registry/store.go:100`): The generic REST storage implementation that most built-in resources embed. Implements CRUD by composing `storage.Interface` with resource-specific strategies (`RESTCreateStrategy`, `RESTUpdateStrategy`, `RESTDeleteStrategy`).
- `StorageDecorator` (`registry/generic/storage_decorator.go:27`): A function type that wraps raw storage creation. The default decorator is `StorageWithCacher()` (`registry/generic/registry/storage_factory.go:36`), which wraps etcd3 storage with the `Cacher`.

---

## 3. End-to-End Request Flow

This section traces a `POST /apis/apps/v1/namespaces/default/deployments` request (creating a Deployment) through the full apiserver code path, citing the exact points where control transfers.

### Phase 1: Filter Chain

The request enters `APIServerHandler.ServeHTTP()` (`handler.go:187`), which delegates to `FullHandlerChain`. The chain was assembled by `DefaultBuildHandlerChain()` (`config.go:1015`). Filters execute in the following order (outermost first at runtime):

```
1. WithAuditInit                              (config.go:1095)
2. WithPanicRecovery                          (config.go:1094)
3. WithMuxAndDiscoveryComplete                (config.go:1093)
4. WithRequestReceivedTimestamp               (config.go:1092)
5. WithRequestInfo                            (config.go:1091)
   → Parses the URL into RequestInfo (verb=create, resource=deployments,
     namespace=default, apiGroup=apps, apiVersion=v1)
6. WithRoutine (conditional)                  (config.go:1089)
7. WithLatencyTrackers                        (config.go:1084)
8. WithHTTPLogging                            (config.go:1083)
9. WithRetryAfter (conditional, shutdown)     (config.go:1081)
10. WithHSTS                                  (config.go:1079)
11. WithCacheControl                          (config.go:1078)
12. WithProbabilisticGoaway (conditional)     (config.go:1076)
13. WithWatchTerminationDuringShutdown        (config.go:1073)
14. WithWaitGroup                             (config.go:1071)
    → Increments NonLongRunningRequestWaitGroup so graceful
      shutdown knows this request is in flight.
15. WithRequestDeadline                       (config.go:1069)
16. WithTimeoutForNonLongRunningRequests      (config.go:1067)
    → Sets a context deadline. The handler runs in a goroutine;
      if it exceeds the timeout, a 504 is returned to the client.
17. WithWarningRecorder                       (config.go:1063)
18. WithCORS                                  (config.go:1059)
19. WithAuthentication                        (config.go:1056)
    → Calls authenticator.Request.AuthenticateRequest(). On success,
      stores user.Info in context. On failure, delegates to
      failedHandler (401 with audit event).
20. WithTracing (conditional)                 (config.go:1052)
21. WithAudit                                 (config.go:1043)
    → Creates audit event, evaluates policy rules, and ensures the
      event is emitted at response time.
22. WithImpersonation / WithConstrainedImpersonation  (config.go:1035-1039)
23. WithPriorityAndFairness / WithMaxInFlightLimit     (config.go:1022-1031)
    → If APF is enabled, classifies the request into a FlowSchema
      and PriorityLevel, potentially queuing it. Otherwise, enforces
      simple concurrency limits (MaxRequestsInFlight /
      MaxMutatingRequestsInFlight).
24. WithAuthorization                         (config.go:1019)
    → Calls authorizer.Authorize(). Returns 403 if not allowed.
```

### Phase 2: Routing (Director)

After the filter chain, control reaches the `director.ServeHTTP()` method (`handler.go:121`). The director iterates over registered `go-restful` WebServices. For our request path `/apis/apps/v1/namespaces/default/deployments`, it matches a WebService rooted at `/apis/apps/v1` and dispatches via `goRestfulContainer.Dispatch()` (`handler.go:145`).

If no WebService matches (e.g., `/healthz`, `/metrics`), the request falls through to `NonGoRestfulMux` (`handler.go:153`).

### Phase 3: REST Handler Execution

The go-restful container matches the route to the handler installed by `registerResourceHandlers()` (`installer.go:287`). For a POST, this is `restfulCreateResource()` (`installer.go:1306`), which wraps `CreateResource()` (`create.go:245`).

Inside `CreateResource()`:

1. **Decode the request body** (create.go, ~line 100-140): Uses the negotiated serializer based on `Content-Type`. The object is deserialized into an internal `runtime.Object`.

2. **Default and validate** (via `CreateStrategy`): The `RESTCreateStrategy` interface (defined in `registry/rest/`) calls `PrepareForCreate`, `Validate`, and `Canonicalize` on the object.

3. **Mutating admission** (~line 203): If the admission controller implements `MutationInterface`, `Admit()` is called. This runs the entire chain of mutating admission plugins (including webhooks) in sequence. Each plugin can modify the object.

4. **Validating admission** (~line 188): `Validate()` runs all validating admission plugins. Errors from all plugins are aggregated and returned.

5. **Field management** (via `FieldManager`, ~line 199): For server-side apply and managed-field tracking, the field manager updates ownership metadata.

6. **Persist to storage**: The handler calls `rest.NamedCreater.Create()`, which resolves to `Store.Create()` (`registry/generic/registry/store.go`). The `Store` constructs the etcd key using `KeyFunc`, then calls `storage.Interface.Create()`.

### Phase 4: Storage Write Path

`storage.Interface.Create()` reaches either the `Cacher` or the raw `etcd3.store`, depending on the decorator.

When using the standard `StorageWithCacher()` decorator:
- **Create goes directly to etcd**: `Cacher` delegates mutations to the underlying `etcd3.store.Create()` (`storage/etcd3/store.go`). The etcd3 implementation encodes the object, applies any configured `value.Transformer` (e.g., encryption at rest), and issues an etcd `PUT` with a lease if TTL is specified.
- **The Cacher's watch cache is updated asynchronously**: A separate goroutine running a `Reflector` watches etcd for changes and pushes events into the `Cacher.incoming` channel, which are then dispatched to all registered `cacheWatcher` instances.

### Phase 5: Response Serialization

After successful persistence, the handler serializes the created object using the negotiated output serializer, sets appropriate HTTP status (201 Created), and writes it to the `http.ResponseWriter`. The filter chain then unwinds—audit events are finalized, wait groups are decremented, and latency metrics are recorded.

### Watch Flow (Variant)

For `GET /api/v1/pods?watch=true`, `ListResource()` (`get.go:171`) detects `opts.Watch == true` at line ~250, calls `rw.Watch()` on the storage to obtain a `watch.Interface`, and hands it to `serveWatchHandler()` (`watch.go:70`). This creates a `WatchServer` (`watch.go:188`) that enters a long-running loop (`HandleHTTP()`, watch.go:235):

```
for {
    select {
    case event := <-watcher.ResultChan():
        encode and flush event to HTTP response
    case <-serverShuttingDown:
        return
    case <-ctx.Done():
        return
    case <-timeout:
        return
    }
}
```

Events arrive from `Cacher.Watch()`, which creates a `cacheWatcher` (`storage/cacher/cache_watcher.go:53`) that filters the shared event stream by the watch's predicate. This fan-out architecture means thousands of watchers share a single etcd watch per resource type.

---

## 4. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs

### 4.1 Watch Cache Consistency

The `Cacher` serves list and watch from an in-memory cache that lags behind etcd by an indeterminate amount. This is a deliberate tradeoff: serving from cache avoids overwhelming etcd with read traffic, but clients can observe stale data.

**Mitigation:** The `ResourceVersion` mechanism provides consistency control. Clients can request `resourceVersion=0` (serve from cache, any version) or `resourceVersion=""` (quorum read from etcd). The `Cacher` tracks the `incomingHWM` (high water mark, `cacher.go:266`) to determine the freshest version available in cache. Bookmark events (`cacheWatcher` states at `cache_watcher.go:37-49`) keep long-running watches informed of progress even when no real events occur.

**Risk:** If the reflector falls behind or is disconnected, the cache may serve increasingly stale data. The `ready` flag (`cacher.go:280`) blocks reads during cache repopulation to prevent serving partial state.

### 4.2 Admission Webhook Failure

Mutating and validating admission webhooks are external HTTP calls. If a webhook endpoint is unreachable:

- **Fail-open vs. fail-closed** is configured per webhook via `failurePolicy`. A `Fail` policy rejects the request; an `Ignore` policy allows it to proceed without webhook evaluation.
- **Timeout:** Webhooks have a configurable timeout (default 10s). A slow webhook blocks the entire request.

**Tradeoff:** Fail-closed provides stronger policy enforcement but can cause cascading unavailability if the webhook backend is down—potentially blocking all mutations in the cluster. Fail-open preserves availability but allows policy violations to slip through.

### 4.3 Request Timeout and Long-Running Requests

`WithTimeoutForNonLongRunningRequests` (`filters/timeout.go:38`) enforces a deadline on regular requests. Long-running requests (watches, port-forwards, exec) are identified by `LongRunningFunc` (`filters/longrunning.go:28`) based on verb and subresource and are **exempted** from this timeout.

**Tradeoff:** Timeout exemption for watches means a misbehaving watch can consume a connection and goroutine indefinitely. The `WithWatchTerminationDuringShutdown` filter (`filters/watch_termination.go:28`) addresses this during graceful shutdown by tracking watches in a wait group and terminating them after a grace period. The `WatchRequestWaitGroup` (`genericapiserver.go:228`) uses rate-limited termination to avoid thundering-herd reconnects.

### 4.4 Priority and Fairness (APF) vs. Max-In-Flight

The server supports two concurrency control mechanisms, selected by whether `FlowControl` is configured (`config.go:1022-1031`):

- **APF** (`filters/priority-and-fairness.go`): Classifies requests into FlowSchemas mapped to PriorityLevels, each with dedicated concurrency shares and queuing. This prevents a burst of low-priority requests (e.g., a runaway controller list-all) from starving high-priority requests (e.g., node heartbeats).
- **Max-In-Flight** (`filters/maxinflight.go:126`): Simple counting semaphore with separate limits for mutating and read-only requests. No queuing—excess requests get 429.

**Tradeoff:** APF provides fairness but adds complexity and latency (queue wait time). Max-In-Flight is simpler but provides no isolation between tenants or request classes.

### 4.5 etcd Storage Pressure

Every mutating request results in an etcd write. The `GuaranteedUpdate` method (`storage/interfaces.go:243`) retries on conflict (stale `resourceVersion`), which can amplify write load under contention. The etcd3 store maintains leases for TTL objects and runs a compactor to reclaim space.

**Limit:** etcd has a default 1.5 MB value size limit and a recommended database size of 8 GB. Large objects (e.g., very large ConfigMaps, deeply nested CRDs) can hit the value size limit. The `maxRequestBodyBytes` field on `GenericAPIServer` (`genericapiserver.go:238`) bounds request body sizes server-side.

### 4.6 Graceful Shutdown Sequence

The shutdown flow (`genericapiserver.go:525-624`) is multi-phased:

1. `stopCh` is closed → `ShutdownInitiated` signal fires → `/readyz` starts returning failure.
2. `ShutdownDelayDuration` elapses → `AfterShutdownDelayDuration` signal fires → load balancers should have drained.
3. Pre-shutdown hooks execute.
4. `NotAcceptingNewRequest` signal fires → new requests get 429 with `Retry-After` (if `ShutdownSendRetryAfter` is true).
5. In-flight non-long-running requests drain → `InFlightRequestsDrained` signal fires.
6. Watch connections terminate (bounded by `ShutdownWatchTerminationGracePeriod`).
7. HTTP server closes with a final `ShutdownTimeout`.
8. `Destroy()` cleans up storage connections.

**Risk:** If `ShutdownDelayDuration` is too short, load balancers may still route traffic to the shutting-down server, causing client errors. If it's too long, rolling updates are slower.

---

## 5. Extension Points, Customization Hooks, and Associated Risks

### 5.1 `BuildHandlerChainFunc`

**Location:** `Config.BuildHandlerChainFunc` (`config.go`, field on `Config` struct ~line 186)

**What it does:** Allows replacing the entire filter chain. The default is `DefaultBuildHandlerChain`. A custom function receives the API handler and `*Config` and must return an `http.Handler`.

**Use case:** Adding custom authentication, injecting request-scoped metadata, or adding custom rate limiting.

**Risk:** Replacing the chain means the operator is responsible for including all security-critical filters (authentication, authorization, audit). Omitting `WithAuthorization` would create an unauthenticated API server. Omitting `WithPanicRecovery` could crash the process on handler panics.

### 5.2 PostStartHooks and PreShutdownHooks

**Location:** `GenericAPIServer.AddPostStartHook()` (`hooks.go:83`), `AddPreShutdownHook()`

**What they do:** PostStartHooks run in separate goroutines after the server begins listening. They receive a `PostStartHookContext` with a loopback client config. Each hook has an associated health check—the server is not healthy until all hooks complete.

**Use case:** Initializing controllers that need the server's own API (e.g., populating discovery, starting informers). The kube-apiserver uses these extensively to bootstrap controllers.

**Risk:** A hook that blocks or panics will either keep the server unhealthy (blocking readiness) or terminate the process (`hooks.go:195-206` runs with crash recovery that fatals on error). Hooks run concurrently with no ordering guarantees, so inter-hook dependencies must be coordinated externally (e.g., via channels or informer cache syncs).

### 5.3 Admission Plugin Registration

**Location:** `admission.Interface` (`admission/interfaces.go:123`), wired via `Config.AdmissionControl`

**What it does:** The admission chain is fully pluggable. Custom plugins implement `MutationInterface`, `ValidationInterface`, or both. They are composed into a `chainAdmissionHandler` (`admission/chain.go:23`).

**Use case:** Enforcing custom business rules, injecting sidecars, validating naming conventions.

**Risk:** Mutating plugins run in order, and later plugins see earlier mutations. This creates implicit coupling between plugins. A mutating plugin that modifies a field validated by a subsequent validating plugin can cause unexpected rejections. The admission chain is also single-threaded per request—a slow plugin blocks the entire request pipeline.

### 5.4 StorageDecorator

**Location:** `StorageDecorator` function type (`registry/generic/storage_decorator.go:27`), passed via `RESTOptions.Decorator`

**What it does:** Wraps storage creation. The default `StorageWithCacher()` (`registry/generic/registry/storage_factory.go:36`) adds the `Cacher` watch cache layer. Custom decorators could add encryption, auditing, or custom caching.

**Use case:** The encryption-at-rest feature uses a `value.Transformer` passed through the storage config rather than the decorator, but the decorator pattern could wrap storage with custom metrics or tracing.

**Risk:** A decorator that introduces latency on the write path directly impacts API response times. A decorator that caches writes without proper invalidation can cause split-brain between what etcd holds and what the API returns.

### 5.5 REST Strategy Interfaces

**Location:** `rest.RESTCreateStrategy`, `rest.RESTUpdateStrategy`, `rest.RESTDeleteStrategy` (defined in `registry/rest/`)

**What they do:** These interfaces allow resource-specific logic to plug into the generic `Store`. Methods like `PrepareForCreate`, `Validate`, `PrepareForUpdate`, and `ValidateUpdate` are called at well-defined points in the CRUD lifecycle.

**Use case:** Every built-in resource (Pod, Deployment, Service, etc.) implements these to enforce its own invariants.

**Risk:** Strategy implementations that are too expensive (e.g., deep-copying large objects unnecessarily, running expensive validation) degrade API latency. The `Store` invokes strategies synchronously and does not set per-strategy timeouts.

### 5.6 DelegationTarget Chain

**Location:** `DelegationTarget` interface (`genericapiserver.go:308`), `GenericAPIServer.delegationTarget` field

**What it does:** Enables composing multiple API servers into a chain. The kube-apiserver binary composes three servers: `apiextensions-apiserver` → `kube-apiserver` → `aggregator`. Each server in the chain installs its own API groups and contributes hooks and health checks.

The `UnprotectedHandler()` method (`genericapiserver.go:339`) returns the delegate's `Director`, allowing the outer server to route unmatched requests to the inner server without re-applying the filter chain.

**Use case:** The aggregator server proxies requests for aggregated API services to their backing API servers. The CRD server (apiextensions) dynamically installs and removes routes as CRDs are created and deleted.

**Risk:** Delegation introduces a request forwarding hop. If the delegate is slow, the outer server's timeout applies. Misconfigured delegation (e.g., two servers claiming the same API group) results in route conflicts and undefined behavior.

### 5.7 The `Decorator` Field on `Store`

**Location:** `Store.Decorator` (`registry/generic/registry/store.go:170`)

**What it does:** An optional function called on every object returned from storage (both individual gets and list items). Unlike storage decorators, this runs *after* persistence, on the returned object.

**Use case:** Adding computed fields that aren't persisted (e.g., a node's allocatable resources derived from capacity minus reservations).

**Risk:** Decorators cannot be watched—the watch stream delivers objects without decoration. This creates an inconsistency between GET and WATCH responses. The docstring explicitly warns about this limitation.

### 5.8 `BeginCreate` / `BeginUpdate` Transaction Hooks

**Location:** `Store.BeginCreate` (`store.go:179`), `Store.BeginUpdate` (`store.go:191`)

**What they do:** Return a commit/revert function that is called at the end of the operation, indicating success or failure. This provides a transaction-like pattern for operations that need side-effects coordinated with persistence.

**Use case:** Allocating IPs during Service creation—if the etcd write fails, the IP allocation is reverted.

**Risk:** The hook runs before `AfterCreate` and `Decorator`. If the commit function has side effects that depend on external state (e.g., an IP allocator), failures in the commit function can leave the system in an inconsistent state.

---

## 6. Operational Implications

### 6.1 Performance

**Watch scalability** is the single most important performance characteristic. The `Cacher` fan-out architecture means that adding watchers is O(1) with respect to etcd load—each additional watcher only adds a `cacheWatcher` goroutine and a channel to the dispatch loop. However, the dispatch loop (`cacher.go`, dispatching logic) holds the `Cacher` lock while iterating watchers, so very high watcher counts (tens of thousands) can create lock contention. The `indexedWatchers` map (`cacher.go:309`) optimizes dispatch by pre-indexing watchers by trigger value, so only watchers matching an event's trigger are considered.

**APF queue depth** directly impacts tail latency. When a priority level's concurrency limit is reached, requests queue. The queue's `HandSize` and `Queues` parameters in the FlowSchema determine fairness characteristics. Misconfiguration (e.g., too few queues) can cause shuffle-sharding hot-spots.

**Serialization cost** is non-trivial for large list responses. The negotiation layer (`handlers/negotiation/negotiate.go`) selects the codec. Protobuf is significantly faster than JSON for large payloads. The `WatchServer` (`watch.go:188`) allocates a `MemoryAllocator` per watch to reduce GC pressure from repeated serialization.

### 6.2 Reliability

**etcd connectivity loss** is the primary single-point-of-failure scenario. The `Cacher` continues serving reads from cache during etcd outages, but writes fail immediately. The `Reflector` backing the cache enters backoff retry. The storage readiness check (`StorageReadinessHook` at `genericapiserver.go:245`, registered via `registerStorageReadinessCheck` at `genericapiserver.go:934`) can signal unreadiness, but this is informational—the server does not self-terminate.

**Request body limits** (`maxRequestBodyBytes` on `GenericAPIServer`) protect against memory exhaustion from oversized payloads. The default is typically 3 MB. Without this, a single malicious POST could OOM the process.

**Panic recovery** (`filters/wrap.go:31`) catches panics in handlers and returns 500 errors rather than crashing. However, panics in filter-chain code *above* the panic recovery filter (e.g., in `WithAuditInit`) will terminate the process.

### 6.3 Maintainability

The library's layered architecture (filters → routing → handlers → storage) makes it possible to modify individual layers independently. However, several maintainability concerns exist:

- **go-restful coupling:** The `Director` (`handler.go:42`) comment explicitly notes the desire to "completely remove gorestful." The dual-path routing (GoRestful vs. NonGoRestful) adds complexity and subtle behavior differences (e.g., 404 handling, content negotiation).
- **Filter ordering sensitivity:** The handler chain in `DefaultBuildHandlerChain()` is order-dependent. Authorization must come after authentication. Timeout must come before the handler that might block. Moving a filter can create security vulnerabilities or correctness bugs, and there are no compile-time guarantees on ordering.
- **Storage interface breadth:** `storage.Interface` (`storage/interfaces.go:169`) has grown to 12+ methods including `CompactRevision()`, `EnableResourceSizeEstimation()`, and `GetCurrentResourceVersion()`. The interface comment acknowledges this is not ideal (`TODO: Remove when storage.Interface will be separate from etc3.store`).

---

## 7. File and Directory Map

The following table lists the most important files referenced in this analysis, organized by subsystem.

### Server Core

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `pkg/server/genericapiserver.go` | `GenericAPIServer`, `APIGroupInfo`, `DelegationTarget`, `InstallAPIGroups()`, lifecycle management |
| `pkg/server/config.go` | `Config` struct, `DefaultBuildHandlerChain()`, `AuthenticationInfo`, `AuthorizationInfo` |
| `pkg/server/handler.go` | `APIServerHandler`, `director`, request routing between GoRestful and NonGoRestful |
| `pkg/server/hooks.go` | `PostStartHookFunc`, hook registration, execution, and health checking |

### Filter Chain

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `pkg/endpoints/filters/authentication.go` | `WithAuthentication()` — extracts user identity |
| `pkg/endpoints/filters/authorization.go` | `WithAuthorization()` — enforces access policy |
| `pkg/server/filters/priority-and-fairness.go` | `WithPriorityAndFairness()` — APF concurrency control |
| `pkg/server/filters/maxinflight.go` | `WithMaxInFlightLimit()` — simple concurrency semaphore |
| `pkg/server/filters/timeout.go` | `WithTimeoutForNonLongRunningRequests()` — request deadlines |
| `pkg/server/filters/wrap.go` | `WithPanicRecovery()` — crash protection |
| `pkg/server/filters/watch_termination.go` | `WithWatchTerminationDuringShutdown()` — graceful watch cleanup |
| `pkg/server/filters/goaway.go` | `WithProbabilisticGoaway()` — HTTP/2 connection rebalancing |
| `pkg/server/filters/cors.go` | `WithCORS()` — cross-origin request handling |

### Endpoint Handlers

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `pkg/endpoints/installer.go` | `APIInstaller`, `registerResourceHandlers()` — route registration |
| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/get.go` | `GetResource()`, `ListResource()` — read operations |
| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/create.go` | `CreateResource()` — object creation |
| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/update.go` | `UpdateResource()` — full object replacement |
| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/patch.go` | `PatchResource()` — partial updates |
| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/delete.go` | `DeleteResource()`, `DeleteCollection()` — deletion |
| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/watch.go` | `WatchServer`, `serveWatchHandler()` — streaming watches |
| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/rest.go` | `RequestScope`, `ConnectResource()` |
| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/negotiation/negotiate.go` | Content-type negotiation |

### Admission

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `pkg/admission/interfaces.go` | `Interface`, `MutationInterface`, `ValidationInterface`, `Attributes` |
| `pkg/admission/chain.go` | `chainAdmissionHandler` — plugin composition |

### Authentication & Authorization

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `pkg/authentication/request/bearertoken/bearertoken.go` | Bearer token authenticator |
| `pkg/authorization/authorizer/interfaces.go` | `Authorizer` interface, `Decision` type, `Attributes` |

### Storage

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `pkg/storage/interfaces.go` | `storage.Interface` — backend contract |
| `pkg/storage/etcd3/store.go` | `etcd3.store` — direct etcd3 implementation |
| `pkg/storage/cacher/cacher.go` | `Cacher` — in-memory watch cache |
| `pkg/storage/cacher/cache_watcher.go` | `cacheWatcher` — per-watch event filtering |
| `pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go` | `Store` — generic CRUD with strategies |
| `pkg/registry/generic/storage_decorator.go` | `StorageDecorator` function type |
| `pkg/registry/generic/registry/storage_factory.go` | `StorageWithCacher()` — default cacher decorator |
| `pkg/registry/generic/options.go` | `RESTOptions`, `RESTOptionsGetter` |
| `pkg/registry/rest/rest.go` | REST storage interfaces (`Getter`, `Lister`, `Creater`, etc.) |

All paths above are relative to `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/`.

# Kubernetes API Server Library: Architecture and Extension Guide

## 1. Subsystem Purpose and Boundaries

The `apiserver` library (`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/`) is a reusable framework for building Kubernetes-style API servers. It provides the full HTTP request lifecycle—authentication, authorization, admission control, serialization, REST semantics, storage, and watch—as composable Go packages. The `kube-apiserver` binary is the primary consumer, but the library is intentionally decoupled so that projects like the aggregated API server, CRD API server, and custom API servers built with `apiserver-builder` can embed and extend it independently.

### Boundaries

The library owns everything *between* the TLS listener and the etcd client. It does **not** own:

- **Upstream:** TLS certificate management, load-balancer health probing, or network policy. These are external to the process.
- **Downstream:** The etcd cluster itself. The library provides an etcd3 storage driver (`pkg/storage/etcd3/`) but treats etcd as a pluggable backend behind the `storage.Interface` abstraction (`pkg/storage/interfaces.go:169`).
- **Peer concerns:** Controller logic, scheduler decisions, and kubelet communication. The API server library is strictly a data-plane gateway for the cluster's persisted state.

### Upstream Dependencies

| Dependency | Role |
|---|---|
| `k8s.io/apimachinery` | Shared type system (`runtime.Object`, `schema.GroupVersionKind`), serialization codecs, field selectors, label selectors |
| `k8s.io/client-go` | Loopback client for self-referential operations (post-start hooks use `LoopbackClientConfig`) |
| `go.etcd.io/etcd/client/v3` | etcd gRPC client used by `pkg/storage/etcd3/store.go` |
| `github.com/emicklei/go-restful/v3` | HTTP routing framework for API group endpoints |
| `k8s.io/kube-openapi` | OpenAPI v2/v3 spec generation and serving |

### Downstream Consumers

Any binary that calls `GenericAPIServer.InstallAPIGroups()` to register resources. The canonical example is `cmd/kube-apiserver`, but the library is also used by the aggregator (`kube-aggregator`) and sample API servers.

---

## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split

The library decomposes into six major subsystems. Each has a clear responsibility boundary and communicates with neighbors through well-defined Go interfaces.

### 2.1 Server Core (`pkg/server/`)

**Responsibility:** Assembles the HTTP handler chain, manages server lifecycle (start, readiness, graceful shutdown), and provides the delegation mechanism for composing multiple API servers.

**Key types:**

- `GenericAPIServer` (`genericapiserver.go:109`): The central server struct. Holds the handler, admission controller, authorizer, post-start hooks, health checks, lifecycle signals, and the delegation target chain.
- `Config` (`config.go:110`): Configuration for building a `GenericAPIServer`. Contains `BuildHandlerChainFunc`, authentication/authorization info, flow control settings, shutdown parameters, and the `RESTOptionsGetter` for storage configuration.
- `APIServerHandler` (`handler.go:42`): Wraps three HTTP handler tiers: `FullHandlerChain` (all filters applied), `GoRestfulContainer` (API routes), and `NonGoRestfulMux` (non-API routes like `/healthz`, `/metrics`). The `Director` (`handler.go:115`) routes between the two backends.
- `DelegationTarget` interface (`genericapiserver.go:308`): Enables chaining of multiple API servers (e.g., kube-apiserver delegates to aggregator delegates to apiextensions-server). Each delegate contributes its own hooks, health checks, and unprotected handlers.

### 2.2 Filter Chain (`pkg/server/filters/`, `pkg/endpoints/filters/`)

**Responsibility:** Implements the ordered chain of HTTP middleware that every request passes through before reaching the REST handler.

These are pure `http.Handler` wrappers composed in `DefaultBuildHandlerChain()` (`config.go:1015`). The chain is assembled inside-out (innermost handler wraps first), so the outermost filter in the source code is the first to execute at runtime.

Filter files are split across two packages:
- `pkg/server/filters/` — infrastructure filters (panic recovery, timeouts, flow control, CORS, GOAWAY)
- `pkg/endpoints/filters/` — API-semantic filters (authentication, authorization, audit, impersonation, request info)

### 2.3 Endpoint Handlers (`pkg/endpoints/`)

**Responsibility:** Maps REST verbs to handler functions, installs API group routes into the go-restful container, and drives content negotiation.

- `APIInstaller` (`installer.go:62`): Given an `APIGroupVersion` with a storage map, generates go-restful `WebService` routes for every resource. The central function `registerResourceHandlers()` (`installer.go:287`) introspects which `rest.*` interfaces each storage implements (e.g., `rest.Creater`, `rest.Lister`, `rest.Watcher`) and registers only the supported verbs.
- Verb handlers in `pkg/endpoints/handlers/`: `GetResource()` / `ListResource()` (`get.go`), `CreateResource()` (`create.go`), `UpdateResource()` (`update.go`), `PatchResource()` (`patch.go`), `DeleteResource()` / `DeleteCollection()` (`delete.go`), `WatchServer` (`watch.go`).
- Content negotiation (`handlers/negotiation/negotiate.go`): Matches `Accept` / `Content-Type` headers to registered serializers (JSON, YAML, Protobuf, CBOR).

### 2.4 Admission Control (`pkg/admission/`)

**Responsibility:** Runs pluggable mutation and validation logic on create, update, and delete operations after authentication and authorization but before persistence.

- `Interface` / `MutationInterface` / `ValidationInterface` (`interfaces.go:123-144`): The core plugin interfaces. A plugin can implement mutation, validation, or both.
- `chainAdmissionHandler` (`chain.go:23`): Chains multiple plugins. `Admit()` runs all mutating plugins in order, short-circuiting on error. `Validate()` runs all validating plugins and aggregates errors.
- Admission is invoked inside endpoint handlers. For example, in `CreateResource()` (`create.go`), mutating admission runs at line ~203 via `mutatingAdmission.Admit()`, and validating admission is wired through `rest.AdmissionToValidateObjectFunc()`.

### 2.5 Authentication & Authorization (`pkg/authentication/`, `pkg/authorization/`, `pkg/endpoints/filters/`)

**Responsibility:** Establish caller identity and enforce access policy.

- **Authentication filter** (`endpoints/filters/authentication.go:46`): `WithAuthentication()` calls `authenticator.Request.AuthenticateRequest()`, stores the resulting `user.Info` in the request context, and strips the `Authorization` header. If authentication fails, the request is forwarded to a `failedHandler` that returns 401.
- **Authorization filter** (`endpoints/filters/authorization.go:53`): `WithAuthorization()` constructs `AttributesRecord` from the request info and user, calls `authorizer.Authorize()`, and returns 403 on `DecisionDeny` or `DecisionNoOpinion`. The `Authorizer` interface (`authorization/authorizer/interfaces.go:83`) returns a three-valued decision: `DecisionAllow`, `DecisionDeny`, `DecisionNoOpinion`.

### 2.6 Storage Layer (`pkg/storage/`, `pkg/registry/`)

**Responsibility:** Provides a key-value abstraction over etcd and an in-memory watch cache for serving list and watch requests efficiently.

- `storage.Interface` (`storage/interfaces.go:169`): The backend contract. Methods: `Create`, `Delete`, `Watch`, `Get`, `GetList`, `GuaranteedUpdate`. Implementations do not know about Kubernetes resource semantics—they operate on opaque keys and `runtime.Object`.
- `etcd3.store` (`storage/etcd3/store.go:80`): Direct etcd3 implementation. Handles codec transformations, lease management, and value encryption via a configurable `value.Transformer`.
- `Cacher` (`storage/cacher/cacher.go:262`): In-memory cache that fronts the etcd3 store. Serves `Watch` and `List` from a ring-buffer `watchCache` populated by a `Reflector`. Mutations are delegated to the underlying `storage.Interface`. This is the component that makes watch scalable—without it, every watch would be a direct etcd watch, which does not scale past a few hundred concurrent watchers.
- `Store` (`registry/generic/registry/store.go:100`): The generic REST storage implementation that most built-in resources embed. Implements CRUD by composing `storage.Interface` with resource-specific strategies (`RESTCreateStrategy`, `RESTUpdateStrategy`, `RESTDeleteStrategy`).
- `StorageDecorator` (`registry/generic/storage_decorator.go:27`): A function type that wraps raw storage creation. The default decorator is `StorageWithCacher()` (`registry/generic/registry/storage_factory.go:36`), which wraps etcd3 storage with the `Cacher`.

---

## 3. End-to-End Request Flow

This section traces a `POST /apis/apps/v1/namespaces/default/deployments` request (creating a Deployment) through the full apiserver code path, citing the exact points where control transfers.

### Phase 1: Filter Chain

The request enters `APIServerHandler.ServeHTTP()` (`handler.go:187`), which delegates to `FullHandlerChain`. The chain was assembled by `DefaultBuildHandlerChain()` (`config.go:1015`). Filters execute in the following order (outermost first at runtime):

```
1. WithAuditInit                              (config.go:1095)
2. WithPanicRecovery                          (config.go:1094)
3. WithMuxAndDiscoveryComplete                (config.go:1093)
4. WithRequestReceivedTimestamp               (config.go:1092)
5. WithRequestInfo                            (config.go:1091)
   → Parses the URL into RequestInfo (verb=create, resource=deployments,
     namespace=default, apiGroup=apps, apiVersion=v1)
6. WithRoutine (conditional)                  (config.go:1089)
7. WithLatencyTrackers                        (config.go:1084)
8. WithHTTPLogging                            (config.go:1083)
9. WithRetryAfter (conditional, shutdown)     (config.go:1081)
10. WithHSTS                                  (config.go:1079)
11. WithCacheControl                          (config.go:1078)
12. WithProbabilisticGoaway (conditional)     (config.go:1076)
13. WithWatchTerminationDuringShutdown        (config.go:1073)
14. WithWaitGroup                             (config.go:1071)
    → Increments NonLongRunningRequestWaitGroup so graceful
      shutdown knows this request is in flight.
15. WithRequestDeadline                       (config.go:1069)
16. WithTimeoutForNonLongRunningRequests      (config.go:1067)
    → Sets a context deadline. The handler runs in a goroutine;
      if it exceeds the timeout, a 504 is returned to the client.
17. WithWarningRecorder                       (config.go:1063)
18. WithCORS                                  (config.go:1059)
19. WithAuthentication                        (config.go:1056)
    → Calls authenticator.Request.AuthenticateRequest(). On success,
      stores user.Info in context. On failure, delegates to
      failedHandler (401 with audit event).
20. WithTracing (conditional)                 (config.go:1052)
21. WithAudit                                 (config.go:1043)
    → Creates audit event, evaluates policy rules, and ensures the
      event is emitted at response time.
22. WithImpersonation / WithConstrainedImpersonation  (config.go:1035-1039)
23. WithPriorityAndFairness / WithMaxInFlightLimit     (config.go:1022-1031)
    → If APF is enabled, classifies the request into a FlowSchema
      and PriorityLevel, potentially queuing it. Otherwise, enforces
      simple concurrency limits (MaxRequestsInFlight /
      MaxMutatingRequestsInFlight).
24. WithAuthorization                         (config.go:1019)
    → Calls authorizer.Authorize(). Returns 403 if not allowed.
```

### Phase 2: Routing (Director)

After the filter chain, control reaches the `director.ServeHTTP()` method (`handler.go:121`). The director iterates over registered `go-restful` WebServices. For our request path `/apis/apps/v1/namespaces/default/deployments`, it matches a WebService rooted at `/apis/apps/v1` and dispatches via `goRestfulContainer.Dispatch()` (`handler.go:145`).

If no WebService matches (e.g., `/healthz`, `/metrics`), the request falls through to `NonGoRestfulMux` (`handler.go:153`).

### Phase 3: REST Handler Execution

The go-restful container matches the route to the handler installed by `registerResourceHandlers()` (`installer.go:287`). For a POST, this is `restfulCreateResource()` (`installer.go:1306`), which wraps `CreateResource()` (`create.go:245`).

Inside `CreateResource()`:

1. **Decode the request body** (create.go, ~line 100-140): Uses the negotiated serializer based on `Content-Type`. The object is deserialized into an internal `runtime.Object`.

2. **Default and validate** (via `CreateStrategy`): The `RESTCreateStrategy` interface (defined in `registry/rest/`) calls `PrepareForCreate`, `Validate`, and `Canonicalize` on the object.

3. **Mutating admission** (~line 203): If the admission controller implements `MutationInterface`, `Admit()` is called. This runs the entire chain of mutating admission plugins (including webhooks) in sequence. Each plugin can modify the object.

4. **Validating admission** (~line 188): `Validate()` runs all validating admission plugins. Errors from all plugins are aggregated and returned.

5. **Field management** (via `FieldManager`, ~line 199): For server-side apply and managed-field tracking, the field manager updates ownership metadata.

6. **Persist to storage**: The handler calls `rest.NamedCreater.Create()`, which resolves to `Store.Create()` (`registry/generic/registry/store.go`). The `Store` constructs the etcd key using `KeyFunc`, then calls `storage.Interface.Create()`.

### Phase 4: Storage Write Path

`storage.Interface.Create()` reaches either the `Cacher` or the raw `etcd3.store`, depending on the decorator.

When using the standard `StorageWithCacher()` decorator:
- **Create goes directly to etcd**: `Cacher` delegates mutations to the underlying `etcd3.store.Create()` (`storage/etcd3/store.go`). The etcd3 implementation encodes the object, applies any configured `value.Transformer` (e.g., encryption at rest), and issues an etcd `PUT` with a lease if TTL is specified.
- **The Cacher's watch cache is updated asynchronously**: A separate goroutine running a `Reflector` watches etcd for changes and pushes events into the `Cacher.incoming` channel, which are then dispatched to all registered `cacheWatcher` instances.

### Phase 5: Response Serialization

After successful persistence, the handler serializes the created object using the negotiated output serializer, sets appropriate HTTP status (201 Created), and writes it to the `http.ResponseWriter`. The filter chain then unwinds—audit events are finalized, wait groups are decremented, and latency metrics are recorded.

### Watch Flow (Variant)

For `GET /api/v1/pods?watch=true`, `ListResource()` (`get.go:171`) detects `opts.Watch == true` at line ~250, calls `rw.Watch()` on the storage to obtain a `watch.Interface`, and hands it to `serveWatchHandler()` (`watch.go:70`). This creates a `WatchServer` (`watch.go:188`) that enters a long-running loop (`HandleHTTP()`, watch.go:235):

```
for {
    select {
    case event := <-watcher.ResultChan():
        encode and flush event to HTTP response
    case <-serverShuttingDown:
        return
    case <-ctx.Done():
        return
    case <-timeout:
        return
    }
}
```

Events arrive from `Cacher.Watch()`, which creates a `cacheWatcher` (`storage/cacher/cache_watcher.go:53`) that filters the shared event stream by the watch's predicate. This fan-out architecture means thousands of watchers share a single etcd watch per resource type.

---

## 4. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs

### 4.1 Watch Cache Consistency

The `Cacher` serves list and watch from an in-memory cache that lags behind etcd by an indeterminate amount. This is a deliberate tradeoff: serving from cache avoids overwhelming etcd with read traffic, but clients can observe stale data.

**Mitigation:** The `ResourceVersion` mechanism provides consistency control. Clients can request `resourceVersion=0` (serve from cache, any version) or `resourceVersion=""` (quorum read from etcd). The `Cacher` tracks the `incomingHWM` (high water mark, `cacher.go:266`) to determine the freshest version available in cache. Bookmark events (`cacheWatcher` states at `cache_watcher.go:37-49`) keep long-running watches informed of progress even when no real events occur.

**Risk:** If the reflector falls behind or is disconnected, the cache may serve increasingly stale data. The `ready` flag (`cacher.go:280`) blocks reads during cache repopulation to prevent serving partial state.

### 4.2 Admission Webhook Failure

Mutating and validating admission webhooks are external HTTP calls. If a webhook endpoint is unreachable:

- **Fail-open vs. fail-closed** is configured per webhook via `failurePolicy`. A `Fail` policy rejects the request; an `Ignore` policy allows it to proceed without webhook evaluation.
- **Timeout:** Webhooks have a configurable timeout (default 10s). A slow webhook blocks the entire request.

**Tradeoff:** Fail-closed provides stronger policy enforcement but can cause cascading unavailability if the webhook backend is down—potentially blocking all mutations in the cluster. Fail-open preserves availability but allows policy violations to slip through.

### 4.3 Request Timeout and Long-Running Requests

`WithTimeoutForNonLongRunningRequests` (`filters/timeout.go:38`) enforces a deadline on regular requests. Long-running requests (watches, port-forwards, exec) are identified by `LongRunningFunc` (`filters/longrunning.go:28`) based on verb and subresource and are **exempted** from this timeout.

**Tradeoff:** Timeout exemption for watches means a misbehaving watch can consume a connection and goroutine indefinitely. The `WithWatchTerminationDuringShutdown` filter (`filters/watch_termination.go:28`) addresses this during graceful shutdown by tracking watches in a wait group and terminating them after a grace period. The `WatchRequestWaitGroup` (`genericapiserver.go:228`) uses rate-limited termination to avoid thundering-herd reconnects.

### 4.4 Priority and Fairness (APF) vs. Max-In-Flight

The server supports two concurrency control mechanisms, selected by whether `FlowControl` is configured (`config.go:1022-1031`):

- **APF** (`filters/priority-and-fairness.go`): Classifies requests into FlowSchemas mapped to PriorityLevels, each with dedicated concurrency shares and queuing. This prevents a burst of low-priority requests (e.g., a runaway controller list-all) from starving high-priority requests (e.g., node heartbeats).
- **Max-In-Flight** (`filters/maxinflight.go:126`): Simple counting semaphore with separate limits for mutating and read-only requests. No queuing—excess requests get 429.

**Tradeoff:** APF provides fairness but adds complexity and latency (queue wait time). Max-In-Flight is simpler but provides no isolation between tenants or request classes.

### 4.5 etcd Storage Pressure

Every mutating request results in an etcd write. The `GuaranteedUpdate` method (`storage/interfaces.go:243`) retries on conflict (stale `resourceVersion`), which can amplify write load under contention. The etcd3 store maintains leases for TTL objects and runs a compactor to reclaim space.

**Limit:** etcd has a default 1.5 MB value size limit and a recommended database size of 8 GB. Large objects (e.g., very large ConfigMaps, deeply nested CRDs) can hit the value size limit. The `maxRequestBodyBytes` field on `GenericAPIServer` (`genericapiserver.go:238`) bounds request body sizes server-side.

### 4.6 Graceful Shutdown Sequence

The shutdown flow (`genericapiserver.go:525-624`) is multi-phased:

1. `stopCh` is closed → `ShutdownInitiated` signal fires → `/readyz` starts returning failure.
2. `ShutdownDelayDuration` elapses → `AfterShutdownDelayDuration` signal fires → load balancers should have drained.
3. Pre-shutdown hooks execute.
4. `NotAcceptingNewRequest` signal fires → new requests get 429 with `Retry-After` (if `ShutdownSendRetryAfter` is true).
5. In-flight non-long-running requests drain → `InFlightRequestsDrained` signal fires.
6. Watch connections terminate (bounded by `ShutdownWatchTerminationGracePeriod`).
7. HTTP server closes with a final `ShutdownTimeout`.
8. `Destroy()` cleans up storage connections.

**Risk:** If `ShutdownDelayDuration` is too short, load balancers may still route traffic to the shutting-down server, causing client errors. If it's too long, rolling updates are slower.

---

## 5. Extension Points, Customization Hooks, and Associated Risks

### 5.1 `BuildHandlerChainFunc`

**Location:** `Config.BuildHandlerChainFunc` (`config.go`, field on `Config` struct ~line 186)

**What it does:** Allows replacing the entire filter chain. The default is `DefaultBuildHandlerChain`. A custom function receives the API handler and `*Config` and must return an `http.Handler`.

**Use case:** Adding custom authentication, injecting request-scoped metadata, or adding custom rate limiting.

**Risk:** Replacing the chain means the operator is responsible for including all security-critical filters (authentication, authorization, audit). Omitting `WithAuthorization` would create an unauthenticated API server. Omitting `WithPanicRecovery` could crash the process on handler panics.

### 5.2 PostStartHooks and PreShutdownHooks

**Location:** `GenericAPIServer.AddPostStartHook()` (`hooks.go:83`), `AddPreShutdownHook()`

**What they do:** PostStartHooks run in separate goroutines after the server begins listening. They receive a `PostStartHookContext` with a loopback client config. Each hook has an associated health check—the server is not healthy until all hooks complete.

**Use case:** Initializing controllers that need the server's own API (e.g., populating discovery, starting informers). The kube-apiserver uses these extensively to bootstrap controllers.

**Risk:** A hook that blocks or panics will either keep the server unhealthy (blocking readiness) or terminate the process (`hooks.go:195-206` runs with crash recovery that fatals on error). Hooks run concurrently with no ordering guarantees, so inter-hook dependencies must be coordinated externally (e.g., via channels or informer cache syncs).

### 5.3 Admission Plugin Registration

**Location:** `admission.Interface` (`admission/interfaces.go:123`), wired via `Config.AdmissionControl`

**What it does:** The admission chain is fully pluggable. Custom plugins implement `MutationInterface`, `ValidationInterface`, or both. They are composed into a `chainAdmissionHandler` (`admission/chain.go:23`).

**Use case:** Enforcing custom business rules, injecting sidecars, validating naming conventions.

**Risk:** Mutating plugins run in order, and later plugins see earlier mutations. This creates implicit coupling between plugins. A mutating plugin that modifies a field validated by a subsequent validating plugin can cause unexpected rejections. The admission chain is also single-threaded per request—a slow plugin blocks the entire request pipeline.

### 5.4 StorageDecorator

**Location:** `StorageDecorator` function type (`registry/generic/storage_decorator.go:27`), passed via `RESTOptions.Decorator`

**What it does:** Wraps storage creation. The default `StorageWithCacher()` (`registry/generic/registry/storage_factory.go:36`) adds the `Cacher` watch cache layer. Custom decorators could add encryption, auditing, or custom caching.

**Use case:** The encryption-at-rest feature uses a `value.Transformer` passed through the storage config rather than the decorator, but the decorator pattern could wrap storage with custom metrics or tracing.

**Risk:** A decorator that introduces latency on the write path directly impacts API response times. A decorator that caches writes without proper invalidation can cause split-brain between what etcd holds and what the API returns.

### 5.5 REST Strategy Interfaces

**Location:** `rest.RESTCreateStrategy`, `rest.RESTUpdateStrategy`, `rest.RESTDeleteStrategy` (defined in `registry/rest/`)

**What they do:** These interfaces allow resource-specific logic to plug into the generic `Store`. Methods like `PrepareForCreate`, `Validate`, `PrepareForUpdate`, and `ValidateUpdate` are called at well-defined points in the CRUD lifecycle.

**Use case:** Every built-in resource (Pod, Deployment, Service, etc.) implements these to enforce its own invariants.

**Risk:** Strategy implementations that are too expensive (e.g., deep-copying large objects unnecessarily, running expensive validation) degrade API latency. The `Store` invokes strategies synchronously and does not set per-strategy timeouts.

### 5.6 DelegationTarget Chain

**Location:** `DelegationTarget` interface (`genericapiserver.go:308`), `GenericAPIServer.delegationTarget` field

**What it does:** Enables composing multiple API servers into a chain. The kube-apiserver binary composes three servers: `apiextensions-apiserver` → `kube-apiserver` → `aggregator`. Each server in the chain installs its own API groups and contributes hooks and health checks.

The `UnprotectedHandler()` method (`genericapiserver.go:339`) returns the delegate's `Director`, allowing the outer server to route unmatched requests to the inner server without re-applying the filter chain.

**Use case:** The aggregator server proxies requests for aggregated API services to their backing API servers. The CRD server (apiextensions) dynamically installs and removes routes as CRDs are created and deleted.

**Risk:** Delegation introduces a request forwarding hop. If the delegate is slow, the outer server's timeout applies. Misconfigured delegation (e.g., two servers claiming the same API group) results in route conflicts and undefined behavior.

### 5.7 The `Decorator` Field on `Store`

**Location:** `Store.Decorator` (`registry/generic/registry/store.go:170`)

**What it does:** An optional function called on every object returned from storage (both individual gets and list items). Unlike storage decorators, this runs *after* persistence, on the returned object.

**Use case:** Adding computed fields that aren't persisted (e.g., a node's allocatable resources derived from capacity minus reservations).

**Risk:** Decorators cannot be watched—the watch stream delivers objects without decoration. This creates an inconsistency between GET and WATCH responses. The docstring explicitly warns about this limitation.

### 5.8 `BeginCreate` / `BeginUpdate` Transaction Hooks

**Location:** `Store.BeginCreate` (`store.go:179`), `Store.BeginUpdate` (`store.go:191`)

**What they do:** Return a commit/revert function that is called at the end of the operation, indicating success or failure. This provides a transaction-like pattern for operations that need side-effects coordinated with persistence.

**Use case:** Allocating IPs during Service creation—if the etcd write fails, the IP allocation is reverted.

**Risk:** The hook runs before `AfterCreate` and `Decorator`. If the commit function has side effects that depend on external state (e.g., an IP allocator), failures in the commit function can leave the system in an inconsistent state.

---

## 6. Operational Implications

### 6.1 Performance

**Watch scalability** is the single most important performance characteristic. The `Cacher` fan-out architecture means that adding watchers is O(1) with respect to etcd load—each additional watcher only adds a `cacheWatcher` goroutine and a channel to the dispatch loop. However, the dispatch loop (`cacher.go`, dispatching logic) holds the `Cacher` lock while iterating watchers, so very high watcher counts (tens of thousands) can create lock contention. The `indexedWatchers` map (`cacher.go:309`) optimizes dispatch by pre-indexing watchers by trigger value, so only watchers matching an event's trigger are considered.

**APF queue depth** directly impacts tail latency. When a priority level's concurrency limit is reached, requests queue. The queue's `HandSize` and `Queues` parameters in the FlowSchema determine fairness characteristics. Misconfiguration (e.g., too few queues) can cause shuffle-sharding hot-spots.

**Serialization cost** is non-trivial for large list responses. The negotiation layer (`handlers/negotiation/negotiate.go`) selects the codec. Protobuf is significantly faster than JSON for large payloads. The `WatchServer` (`watch.go:188`) allocates a `MemoryAllocator` per watch to reduce GC pressure from repeated serialization.

### 6.2 Reliability

**etcd connectivity loss** is the primary single-point-of-failure scenario. The `Cacher` continues serving reads from cache during etcd outages, but writes fail immediately. The `Reflector` backing the cache enters backoff retry. The storage readiness check (`StorageReadinessHook` at `genericapiserver.go:245`, registered via `registerStorageReadinessCheck` at `genericapiserver.go:934`) can signal unreadiness, but this is informational—the server does not self-terminate.

**Request body limits** (`maxRequestBodyBytes` on `GenericAPIServer`) protect against memory exhaustion from oversized payloads. The default is typically 3 MB. Without this, a single malicious POST could OOM the process.

**Panic recovery** (`filters/wrap.go:31`) catches panics in handlers and returns 500 errors rather than crashing. However, panics in filter-chain code *above* the panic recovery filter (e.g., in `WithAuditInit`) will terminate the process.

### 6.3 Maintainability

The library's layered architecture (filters → routing → handlers → storage) makes it possible to modify individual layers independently. However, several maintainability concerns exist:

- **go-restful coupling:** The `Director` (`handler.go:42`) comment explicitly notes the desire to "completely remove gorestful." The dual-path routing (GoRestful vs. NonGoRestful) adds complexity and subtle behavior differences (e.g., 404 handling, content negotiation).
- **Filter ordering sensitivity:** The handler chain in `DefaultBuildHandlerChain()` is order-dependent. Authorization must come after authentication. Timeout must come before the handler that might block. Moving a filter can create security vulnerabilities or correctness bugs, and there are no compile-time guarantees on ordering.
- **Storage interface breadth:** `storage.Interface` (`storage/interfaces.go:169`) has grown to 12+ methods including `CompactRevision()`, `EnableResourceSizeEstimation()`, and `GetCurrentResourceVersion()`. The interface comment acknowledges this is not ideal (`TODO: Remove when storage.Interface will be separate from etc3.store`).

---

## 7. File and Directory Map

The following table lists the most important files referenced in this analysis, organized by subsystem.

### Server Core

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `pkg/server/genericapiserver.go` | `GenericAPIServer`, `APIGroupInfo`, `DelegationTarget`, `InstallAPIGroups()`, lifecycle management |
| `pkg/server/config.go` | `Config` struct, `DefaultBuildHandlerChain()`, `AuthenticationInfo`, `AuthorizationInfo` |
| `pkg/server/handler.go` | `APIServerHandler`, `director`, request routing between GoRestful and NonGoRestful |
| `pkg/server/hooks.go` | `PostStartHookFunc`, hook registration, execution, and health checking |

### Filter Chain

| `pkg/endpoints/filters/authentication.go` | `WithAuthentication()` — extracts user identity |
| `pkg/endpoints/filters/authorization.go` | `WithAuthorization()` — enforces access policy |
| `pkg/server/filters/priority-and-fairness.go` | `WithPriorityAndFairness()` — APF concurrency control |
| `pkg/server/filters/maxinflight.go` | `WithMaxInFlightLimit()` — simple concurrency semaphore |
| `pkg/server/filters/timeout.go` | `WithTimeoutForNonLongRunningRequests()` — request deadlines |
| `pkg/server/filters/wrap.go` | `WithPanicRecovery()` — crash protection |
| `pkg/server/filters/watch_termination.go` | `WithWatchTerminationDuringShutdown()` — graceful watch cleanup |
| `pkg/server/filters/goaway.go` | `WithProbabilisticGoaway()` — HTTP/2 connection rebalancing |
| `pkg/server/filters/cors.go` | `WithCORS()` — cross-origin request handling |

### Endpoint Handlers

| `pkg/endpoints/installer.go` | `APIInstaller`, `registerResourceHandlers()` — route registration |
| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/get.go` | `GetResource()`, `ListResource()` — read operations |
